messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3. Quality and efficiency of the implementation  
3.1 Work plan and 
resources  
3.1.1. Overall structure 
of our work plan  
Our project workplan is composed of 8 work packages. WP1-4 focus on specific aspects of our RETAIN recycling chain, being logistics, repair/reuse/remanufacture, deconfectioning, recycling, and conversion of recyclates into value added products. WP5 is the actual demonstration part of our project, whereas WP6-7 are supporting tasks. WP8 finally covers our project management and coordination of RETAIN.  
WP1 (M1-M24) focuses on logistics, traceability, monitoring and sorting. We, as lead partner IRIS, partner VEOLIA, and several other partners will develop and implement a strict gate control procedure that distinguishes between various tarp qualities. The development and integration of a digital product passport for tarp will be an important part of the gate control procedure. WP2 (M1-M30) deals with repair, reuse and remanufacturing, and deconfectioning of post-production and EOL tarps. We, as lead partner VWERVEN and partner EKOTREND, will develop a (semi)automized deconfectioning system to remove eyelets, buckets, zippers and other functionalities from EOL tarps. We, as partners SIOEN, SF and several other partners will study repair/reuse/remanufacturing practices to increase the lifetime of tarps. In WP3 (M1-M30) we will study the actual recycling of tarps. Various mechanical recycling (EKOTREND, KKF, VWERVEN) and solvent-based recycling (SF and CTB) methodologies will be investigated and prepared for demonstration in WP5. Furthermore, we will develop selective extraction of valuable additives from tarps (AIMPLAS), and strategies definition and development of a decision support tool for recycling processes (RINA-C). Various other partners will be active in this WP under the leadership of partner CTB. WP4 (M1-M39) covers the conversion of recycled and biobased materials into high value products, producing new tarps through extrusion coating/calendaring as executed by lead partner SIOEN and partner MEHLER. Various other partners such as RENO, BIG, SATTLER and SF will apply plasticizers, PVC and other recyclates into their products. Finally, we, as partner CTB and some other partners will focus on standardization aspects related to tarp recycling. In WP5 (M19-M42) we will execute the actual demonstration of circularity for complex PVC based value chains in three EU regions. We, as lead partner VEOLIA, will coordinate this work. This WP covers 3 main activities: demonstration of the gate control process, demonstration of recycling processes and technologies, and uptake of recycled products. In the final stage of our project, we will assess the RETAIN recycling chain and the demos to conclude on the technical and economic aspects of the RETAIN recycling chain. WP6 (M19-M42), led by partner RINA-C, will focus on sustainability assessment studies. In WP7 (M1-M42) we, as lead partner EuPC and all other partners, will execute all activities related to market acceptance, uptake and deployment. This WP includes dissemination, communication, exploitation and training & learning activities. Finally, in WP8 (M1-M42) all our project management and coordination activities will take place.  

 
 
3.1.2. Timing of the Work Packages and main tasks - Gantt Chart  
  
 
MS4 - GO for screening demo activities  
MS5 - GO for recycling demo activities, relying on the developed options for remanufacturing, deconfectioning, recycling and ecodesign  
MS6 - End of experimental demo activities, switch focus on evaluation and finalisation of documentation  

 
 
MS7 - Ready for ‘Handover’, i.e. to start the beyond RETAIN phase

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3 Summary Specific Needs Expected Results D & E & C Measures • Current SAF supply is low at <0.05% of total EU aviation fuel use. To meet sustainability targets, our SAF supply must be increased (~2.3 Million Tonnes by 2030, 28.6 MT by 2050). • SAFs are currently more expensive than fossil-based jet fuel. • Algae biofuels are currently not sustainable and cost-competitive due to a range of factors: - High biomass production cost (& limited feedstock availability) due to lack of access to cheap and abundant supply of nutrients. - Culture crash due to bacterial contamination - High biorefinery cost due to high energy requirements associated with cell disruption and high solvent requirements. - Low fuel conversion efficiency due to limited understanding of the thermochemical routes for conversion of biomass components to SAFs and the required purification steps. A more sustainable, cost-competitive and efficient process for the production of algae SAFs via technologies that enable production of microalgae on cheap and abundant CO2 and waste nutrients from the AD sector: - Autotrophic growth strategy on digestate - Membrane system for CO2 delivery to microalgae culture - In-line sensor for bacterial detection/enumeration - UV irradiation system for bacterial control - Low-energy cell disruption based on autolysis - Lipid extraction strategy to minimise solvent usage - Multifunctional catalysts with H2 looping route for conversion of algal lipids to HEFA-SPK - Algae-specific HEFA-SPK purification method - Route for the conversion of microalgae biomass residue to TCR-HDO oil (kerosene mixture) - Algae-specific TCR-HDO purification method - Pilot plant for full-system process (TRL4 /5) of converting digestate to microalgae-based aviation fuel - Process simulations, business-modelling, life-cycle analysis and policy assessment/recommendation. Dissemination measures: 20+ Industry Workshops, networking events, webinars, and seminars; Workshops at pilot-scale algae fuel facility on AD site; 10+ open-access publications; >12 presentations and >2 conference workshops; Microalgae Fuel Summer Research School; Technical brochures; Policy white paper; SusAlgaeFuel Final Conference. Exploitation measures: ■ IP protection for new technologies followed by open-access publications and open-science repository according to Data Management Plan; ■ BMCs and Business/commercialisation plans; ■ Policy white paper; ■ RSB certification Communication measures: SusAlgaeFuel public website and social-media channels; Communication training programme; Promotional contents (infographics, leaflets, posters, project videos), Journalistic content (e.g. interviews, press release); Women-in-Science campaign. Target Groups Outcomes Impacts - AD operators as technology adopters - Algae producers as technology providers - Fuel/SAF producers - Aviation sector: Aircraft manufacturers; Leasing facilities; Airports & airlines - By-product users: Biofertiliser & feed producers - Sensor manufacturers - Fuel certification authorities - Industry associations - Academic community / researchers - EU projects in related area - Policymakers and MPs (national & EU^) - Investors in green technology - University students (undergraduate, master, PhD) - General public/air passengers - Further innovative/disruptive research in microalgae and SAF production on the basis of our findings, leading to IP generation, licenses, and spin-outs. - Commercialisation plan for post-project scale up of our SusAlgaeFuel process & adoption by SAF producers, AD operators & businesses along aviation fuel supply chain - Robust mechanisms and research to increase the scale and reduce the cost of algal biofuels to <35 €/MWh by 2030 according to SET plan 8 mandate, increasing the efficiency, and decreasing GHG emissions of our algae SAFs. - Positive policy changes at national and EU levels in relation to SAF adoption, phasing out of fossil fuels and creating subsidies/incentives for SAF commercialization - Greater awareness of the importance of SAF amongst the general public, with an effective focus on air passengers - Scientific and technological: Groundbreaking innovative approaches that work in tandem to enable a more cost-competitive, efficient, and circular production of microalgae SAFs, complemented with process simulations, techno-economic and LCA to assess scalability and identify process improvements. - Energy security in the EU and economic: Delivering world’s 1st microalgae HEFA-SPK and TCR-HDO fuels tested for compliance to ASTM jet-fuel specifications as microalgae substitute aviation fuel with potential to annually treat 172 Mt of digestate for the production of 1 Mt of HEFA-SPK (equivalent to 20% of Europe’s projected SAF requirements of 5 Mt in 2030). [HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels] 30 3 QUALITY AND EFFICIENCY OF IMPLEMENTATION 3.1. Work Plan and Resources Our SusAlgaeFuel project is divided into eight interconnected WPs which work to complement each other in delivering an integrated technology, business model, sustainability and policy solution for algal aviation fuel development in the EU. The interconnectivity between the WPs is illustrated in Figure 3.1a. WPs 1-4 will provide core technology development at bench scale, while WP5 will use the information obtained in these WPs to assess commercial and socio-environmental scalability of the system. Each WP is further divided into several Tasks to address specific objectives. In WP6, the processes developed in WPs 1-4, complemented with process optimisation based on WP5, will be transferred to pilot scale to demonstrate scalability, culminating in the production of sufficient SAF for ASTM compliance test at the end of our project lifetime. We will adopt a staggered approach, with WP6 starting only in Month 25 (2nd phase of our project), allowing investigation in WPs 1-4 to reach sufficient maturity prior to scale-up. WP7 provides blueprint for results dissemination, commercialisation and policy guidance. Figure 3.1a: Our SusAlgaeFuel approach and interconnectivity between WPs Management Structure: The Executive Board (EB) assumes the overarching leadership for our project. It decides on the general policy and strategic orientations of our project to ensure that milestones and deliverables are achieved according to the timeline. The EB is responsible for monitoring the overall progress of our project and designing corrective actions or authorising the execution of risk mitigation strategies when significant project delay/deviation is observed. The EB comprises all WP leaders and our project coordinator. The experienced Project Coordinator (UCD) has the overall responsibility to manage and execute our project. They also act as an intermediary between the European Commission, Advisory Board (which currently consists of Boeing and CityJet), EB and the rest of our consortium. WP Leaders are in charge of: (a) implementing decisions taken by the EB for their WPs, (b) coordinating the activities within their WPs and monitoring progress, (c) reporting to our Project Coordinator, and (d) organizing and chairing online WP meetings. The Project Office (PO) at UCD will manage the daily logistics of coordination activities on behalf of our Project Coordinator, including the organisation of on-site consortium meetings at M1, M18, M36 and M48 Table 3.1a: List of Work Packages WP No. WP Title Lead Part.No Lead Partner Person-Months Start Month End Month 1 High-density cultivation through biogenic CO2 capture and waste utilisation 1 UCD 39.5 1 36 2 Sensor system for bacterial detection and culture purification 3 IRIS 42.0 1 36 3 Cascading biorefinery for biomass purification 5 KIT 63.0 1 36 4 High-efficiency fuel conversion and purification 6 UCAM 70.7 1 36 5 Process integration and whole life-cycle analysis 8 SFS 38.0 13 48 6 Pilot scale-up, fuel testing and digital twin 3 A4F 154.0 25 48 7 Communication, policy and exploitation roadmap 9 ESCI 46.7 1 48 8 Project coordination 1 UCD 29.7 1 48 [HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels] 31 Figure 3.1b: Gantt chart for our SusAlgaeFuel Milestones (ML) and Deliverables (D) M2 M4 M6 M8 M10 M12 M14 M16 M18 M20 M22 M24 M26 M28 M30 M32 M34 M36 M38 M40 M42 M44 M46 M48 ML1.1, D1.1 ML D D1.2 D ML1.2 ML D1.3 D D.2.1 D D2.2 D D2.3 D ML3.1, D3.1 ML D ML3.2, D3.2 ML D ML4.1, D4.1 ML D ML4.2, D4.2 ML D ML4.3, D4.3 ML D ML4.4, D4.4 ML D ML4.5, D4.5 ML D ML5.1, D5.1 ML D D5.2 ML D ML6.1, ML6.2 ML ML ML6.3, ML6.4, D6.1, D6.2 ML D ML D ML6.5, D6.3 ML D D6.4 D D7.1, D
